title: Code of law

infobox.value: or **legal code**, is a systematic collection of [statutes](/wiki/Statute "Statute"). It is a type of legislation that purports to exhaustively cover a complete system of laws or a particular area of law as it existed at the time the code was enacted, by a process of [codification](/wiki/Codification_(law) "Codification (law)").[[1]](#cite_note-1) Though the process and motivations for codification are similar in different [common law](/wiki/Common_law#History "Common law") and [civil law](/wiki/Civil_law_(legal_system) "Civil law (legal system)") systems, their usage is different.

[Chisholm, Hugh](/wiki/Hugh_Chisholm "Hugh Chisholm"), ed. (1911). ["Code"](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/1911_Encyclop%C3%A6dia_Britannica/Code). *[Encyclopædia Britannica](/wiki/Encyclop%C3%A6dia_Britannica_Eleventh_Edition "Encyclopædia Britannica Eleventh Edition")*. Vol. 6 (11th ed.). Cambridge University Press. pp. 632–634.
["Law code | Ancient Legal Systems & Modern Legislation"](https://www.britannica.com/topic/law-code). *Britannica*. Retrieved 2023-12-08.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Legislation that purports to cover a complete system of laws

This article is about exhaustive legislations. For municipal regulations, see [legal code (municipal)](/wiki/Legal_code_(municipal) "Legal code (municipal)"). For the television series, see [Code of Law (TV series)](/wiki/Code_of_Law "Code of Law").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Code_Civil_1804.png/250px-Code_Civil_1804.png)](/wiki/File:Code_Civil_1804.png)

First page of the 1804 original edition of the [Napoleonic Code](/wiki/Napoleonic_Code "Napoleonic Code")

A **code of law**, also called a **law code** or **legal code**, is a systematic collection of [statutes](/wiki/Statute "Statute"). It is a type of legislation that purports to exhaustively cover a complete system of laws or a particular area of law as it existed at the time the code was enacted, by a process of [codification](/wiki/Codification_(law) "Codification (law)").[[1]](#cite_note-1) Though the process and motivations for codification are similar in different [common law](/wiki/Common_law#History "Common law") and [civil law](/wiki/Civil_law_(legal_system) "Civil law (legal system)") systems, their usage is different.

In a civil law country, a code of law typically exhaustively covers the complete system of law, such as civil law or [criminal law](/wiki/Criminal_law "Criminal law").[*[further explanation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]

By contrast, in a common law country with legislative practices in the [English tradition](/wiki/English_law#Common_law "English law"), codes modify the existing common law only to the extent of its express or implicit provision, but otherwise leaves the common law intact. In the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") and other common law countries that have adopted similar legislative practices, a code of law is a standing body of statute law on a particular area, which is added to, subtracted from, or otherwise modified by individual legislative enactments.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Code_of_law&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [List of ancient legal codes](/wiki/List_of_ancient_legal_codes "List of ancient legal codes")

The legal code was a common feature of the legal systems of the ancient Middle East. Tablets discovered in the ancient city of [Ebla](/wiki/Ebla "Ebla") (Tell Mardikh in modern-day [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria")) provide the earliest known evidence of a law code, dating back to 2400 BC.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) In addition, The UrukAgina Law Code (2380–2360 BC),[[4]](#cite_note-4) the [Sumerian](/wiki/Sumer "Sumer") [Code of Ur-Nammu](/wiki/Code_of_Ur-Nammu "Code of Ur-Nammu") (c. 2100–2050 BC), [the Code of Eshnunna](/wiki/Laws_of_Eshnunna "Laws of Eshnunna") (approximately 100 years before Lipit-Ishtar),[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) the [Code of Lipit-Ishtar](/wiki/Code_of_Lipit-Ishtar "Code of Lipit-Ishtar") (1934–1924 BC),[[7]](#cite_note-7) and the [Babylonian](/wiki/Babylon "Babylon") [Code of Hammurabi](/wiki/Code_of_Hammurabi "Code of Hammurabi") (c. 1760 BC), are among the earliest and best preserved legal codes,[[8]](#cite_note-8) originating from [Sumer](/wiki/Sumer "Sumer"), [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia") (now [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq "Iraq")).

In the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire"), a number of codes were developed, such as the [Twelve Tables](/wiki/Twelve_Tables "Twelve Tables") of Roman law (first compiled in 450 BC) and the *[Corpus Juris Civilis](/wiki/Corpus_Juris_Civilis "Corpus Juris Civilis")* of Justinian, also known as the Justinian Code (429–534 AD). However, these law codes did not exhaustively describe the Roman legal system. The Twelve Tables were limited in scope, and most legal doctrines were developed by the *[pontifices](/wiki/Pontifex "Pontifex")*, who interpreted the tables to deal with situations far beyond what is contained therein. The Justinian Code collected together existing legal material at the time.

In ancient [China](/wiki/China "China"), the first comprehensive criminal code was the [Tang Code](/wiki/Tang_Code "Tang Code"), created in 624 AD in the [Tang dynasty](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty"). This, and subsequent imperial codes, formed the basis for the penal system of both China and other East Asian states under its cultural influence. The last and best preserved imperial code is the [Great Qing Legal Code](/wiki/Great_Qing_Legal_Code "Great Qing Legal Code"), created in 1644 upon the founding of the [Qing dynasty](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty"). This code was the exclusive and exhaustive statement of Chinese law between 1644 and 1912. Though it was in form a criminal code, large parts of the code dealt with civil law matters and the settlement of civil disputes. The code ceased its operation upon the fall of the Qing dynasty in 1912, but significant provisions remained in operation in [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong") until well into the 1970s due to a peculiar interaction between it and the [British](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") common law system.

In Europe, [Roman law](/wiki/Roman_law "Roman law"), especially the *[Corpus Juris Civilis](/wiki/Corpus_Juris_Civilis "Corpus Juris Civilis")*, became the basis of the legal systems of many countries. Roman law was either adopted by legislation (becoming [positive law](/wiki/Positive_law "Positive law")), or through processing by jurists. The accepted Roman law is usually then codified and forms part of the central Code. The codification movement gathered pace after the rise of [nation-states](/wiki/Nation-state "Nation-state") after the [Treaty of Westphalia](/wiki/Peace_of_Westphalia "Peace of Westphalia"). Prominent national [civil codes](/wiki/Civil_code "Civil code") include the [French](/wiki/France "France") [Napoleonic Code (*code civil*)](/wiki/Napoleonic_Code "Napoleonic Code") of 1804, the [Austrian](/wiki/Austria "Austria") civil code ([Allgemeines Bürgerliches Gesetzbuch](/wiki/Allgemeines_b%C3%BCrgerliches_Gesetzbuch "Allgemeines bürgerliches Gesetzbuch")) of 1812, the [German](/wiki/Germany "Germany") civil code (*[Bürgerliches Gesetzbuch](/wiki/B%C3%BCrgerliches_Gesetzbuch "Bürgerliches Gesetzbuch")*) of 1900 and the [Swiss codes](/wiki/Swiss_Civil_Code "Swiss Civil Code"). The European codifications of the 1800s influenced the [codification](/wiki/Legal_history_of_the_Catholic_Church "Legal history of the Catholic Church") of [Catholic canon law](/wiki/Canon_law_(Catholic_Church) "Canon law (Catholic Church)")[[9]](#cite_note-9) resulting in the [1917 Code of Canon Law](/wiki/1917_Code_of_Canon_Law "1917 Code of Canon Law") which was replaced by the [1983 Code of Canon Law](/wiki/1983_Code_of_Canon_Law "1983 Code of Canon Law") and whose Eastern counterpart is the [Code of Canons of the Eastern Churches](/wiki/Code_of_Canons_of_the_Eastern_Churches "Code of Canons of the Eastern Churches").

Meanwhile, [African](/wiki/Africa "Africa") civilizations developed their own legal traditions, sometimes codifying them through consistent oral tradition, as illustrated e.g. by the [Kouroukan Fouga](/wiki/Kouroukan_Fouga "Kouroukan Fouga"), a [charter](/wiki/Charter "Charter") proclaimed by the [Mali Empire](/wiki/Mali_Empire "Mali Empire") in 1222–1236, enumerating regulations in both constitutional and civil matters, and transmitted to this day by [griots](/wiki/Griot "Griot") under oath.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

The Continental civil law tradition spread around the world along with European cultural and military dominance in recent centuries. During the [Meiji Restoration](/wiki/Meiji_Restoration "Meiji Restoration"), [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") adopted a new [Civil Code](/wiki/Civil_Code_of_Japan "Civil Code of Japan") (1898), based primarily on the French civil code and influenced by the German code. After the [Xinhai Revolution](/wiki/Xinhai_Revolution "Xinhai Revolution") of 1911 in China, the new [Republic of China](/wiki/Republic_of_China_(1912%E2%80%931949) "Republic of China (1912–1949)") government abandoned the imperial code tradition and instead adopted a new [civil code](/wiki/Civil_code "Civil code") strongly influenced by the German *[Bürgerliches Gesetzbuch](/wiki/B%C3%BCrgerliches_Gesetzbuch "Bürgerliches Gesetzbuch")*, and also influenced by the Japanese code. This new tradition has been largely maintained in the legal system of the [People's Republic of China](/wiki/People%27s_Republic_of_China "People's Republic of China") since 1949.

Meanwhile, codifications also became more common in [common law](/wiki/Common_law "Common law") systems. For example, a [criminal code](/wiki/Criminal_code "Criminal code") is found in a number of common law jurisdictions in [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") and the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas"), and continues to be debated in [England](/wiki/England "England").

In the Americas, the influence of Continental legal codes has manifested itself in two ways. In civil law jurisdictions, legal codes in the Continental tradition are common. In common law jurisdictions, however, there has been a strong trend towards codification.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The result of such codification, however, is not always a legal code as found in civil law jurisdictions. For example, the [California Civil Code](/wiki/California_Civil_Code "California Civil Code") largely codifies common law doctrine and is very different in form and content from all other civil codes.

## Civil code

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Code_of_law&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Civil code")]

A [civil code](/wiki/Civil_code "Civil code") typically forms the core of [civil law](/wiki/Civil_law_(legal_system) "Civil law (legal system)") systems. The legal code typically covers exhaustively the entire system of private law.

## Criminal code

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Code_of_law&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Criminal code")]

A [criminal code](/wiki/Criminal_code "Criminal code") or [penal code](/wiki/Penal_code "Penal code") is a common feature in many legal systems. Codification of the criminal law allows the criminal law to be more accessible and more democratically made and amended.
